events.event_label: Papillae contain polyphenolics, callose and extensins
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p014.s001: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. PMC4125134.p014.s002: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins. PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

events.event_label: chalcone synthase, chalcone isomerase and chitinase gene are PTI indicator genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p093.s001: Tobacco is a well-established model plant for plant-microbe interaction studies, especially in the field of plant-bacterial interactions and a good model plant of the Solanaceae family. PMC4125134.p093.s002: Plant science results should not be generalized incautiously, as differences between plant-groups always occur. PMC4125134.p093.s003: For example in the study of Jakobek and co-workers , bean was used as a model plant and chalcone synthase and chalcone isomerase as indicator genes of PTI, besides a PAL and a chitinase gene. PMC4125134.p093.s004: However the first two indicator genes seem to be specific for leguminous PTI, as they are activated in Medicago as well but not in Arabidopsis and tobacco (Szatmari et al. unpublished).

events.event_label: OMT and F5H participate in phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well. PMC4125134.p071.s005: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL). PMC4125134.p071.s006: Activation of the genes encoding the enzymes of the next stages of the pathway, such as ortho-methyltransferases (OMT) and ferulate-5-hydroxylase (F5H) was also significant. PMC4125134.p071.s007: Three isoenzymes of the peroxidases that oxidize the aromatic alcoholic compounds into lignins were also activated.

events.event_label: hrcC mutant abolishes HR and induces PTI‑related genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p017.s001: Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv. syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off. PMC4125134.p017.s002: The hrcC mutant is especially useful for investigating PTI, as its ability to induce HR in tobacco is completely abolished, while the induced PTI can nicely be detected by an HR-inhibition test, and it is also a potent elicitor of PTI-related genes.

events.event_label: PTI and BR associate with resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

events.event_label: PAL converts phenylalanine to cinnamate, which is converted to p‑coumarate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p111.s006: However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control. PMC4125134.p111.s007: A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate. PMC4125134.p111.s008: In the case of P. syringae hrcC- bacteria a higher level of C4H induction might have occurred, with which PAL could not keep up completely, therefore a relative decrease in cinnamate occurred. PMC4125134.p111.s009: We do not know however if there are other enzymes involved in converting cinnamate into different derivatives, for example into salicylic acid. PMC4125134.p111.s010: Therefore the exact enzymatic mechanisms creating the measured changes in the two phenolic acids will require more studies, for example a higher scale metabolic profiling experiment, preferably including a time series with earlier time points than the 6 hpi samples used here, and/or also applying higher concentrations of flg22.

PMC4125134.p076.s001: To assess if disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway causes a noticeable change in the efficiency of PTI we chose to inhibit C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s002: This enzyme, second just after PAL, catalyzes a rate limiting reaction, causing a bottleneck effect, so its inhibition will have an impact on all further reactions. PMC4125134.p076.s003: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s004: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta . PMC4125134.p076.s009: Only when the incompatible P. syringae pv. syringae 61 was co-infiltrated with PIP (i.e. 0 hpi) did we detect a lack of HR, possibly because of inhibition of bacterial activity by PIP. PMC4125134.p076.s010: Therefore after considering several possible C4H inhibitors from literature, PIP seemed to be the most suitable for our experiments because of its selective and quasi-irreversible nature.

events.event_label: PTI confers resistance and suppresses HR
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

events.event_label: PTI activates phenylpropanoid pathway genes (PAL, C4H, 4CL, OMT, F5H)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p071.s001: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library. PMC4125134.p071.s002: Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well. PMC4125134.p071.s005: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL). PMC4125134.p071.s006: Activation of the genes encoding the enzymes of the next stages of the pathway, such as ortho-methyltransferases (OMT) and ferulate-5-hydroxylase (F5H) was also significant. PMC4125134.p071.s007: Three isoenzymes of the peroxidases that oxidize the aromatic alcoholic compounds into lignins were also activated.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s001: Cinnamic acid was present at a basic level of cca. PMC4125134.p086.s002: 11 nmol/g fresh weight in our samples, as seen in DMSO controls (Figure 6A). PMC4125134.p086.s003: This level was generally elevated upon any type of treatment with the C4H inhibitor PIP, either alone or combined with the PTI elicitors. PMC4125134.p086.s004: Elevation compared to control (DMSO) treatment was not significant in the case of PIP treatment alone, but it was definitely significant when PIP was combined with either PTI elicitor.

PMC4125134.p090.s003: Values in Figure 6B were normalized against actin and water treated control, like in the other RT-PCR measurements. PMC4125134.p090.s004: In general, we saw again that P. s. hrcC- caused stronger activation than flg22. PMC4125134.p090.s005: In the case of 5 out of 7 phenylpropanoid genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), addition of PIP to the PTI elicitors caused a slight elevation in gene activity. PMC4125134.p090.s006: Interestingly, these 5 genes are the closer ones to phenylalanine, i.e. to the origin of the phenylpropanoid pathway, as can be seen in Figure 4. PMC4125134.p090.s007: Even PIP on its own caused a slight elevation in activities of the first 4 PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL), of which transcription of the C4H gene was most elevated (cca. 3 times).

PMC4125134.p098.s001: Unraveling the signaling pathways involved in PTI would bring us closer to the possibility of breeding or engineering plants with a higher capacity of PTI. PMC4125134.p098.s002: Some authors have already made important steps towards this task. PMC4125134.p098.s003: Asai et al. followed a complete MAPK cascade in Arabidopsis from the perception of the MAMP flagellin by its receptor-like-kinase-type receptor FLS2 to the corresponding transcription factors. PMC4125134.p098.s004: However, genetic evidence later showed that the first element, MEKK1 in fact belongs to another module: MEKK1-MKK1-MPK4.

PMC4125134.p101.s009: At 3 hpi each investigated phenylpropanoid gene was activated significantly, while at 6 hpi only two of them: POX (C72) and F5H (C39). PMC4125134.p101.s010: One probable reason for this difference might be that whole bacterial cells contain many other PTI elicitors resulting in a stronger PTI reaction than flagellin alone. PMC4125134.p101.s011: Higher concentrations of flagellin might also trigger stronger responses, which we did not investigate at present. PMC4125134.p101.s012: Still another explanation might be that response to flg22 occurs much quicker, so that peaks of gene activation might occur earlier.

PMC4125134.p103.s001: It was interesting that genes of reactions leading to lignin formation were overrepresented while branches to salicylic acid, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and stilbenes were not present at all (Figure 4). PMC4125134.p103.s002: This was not unexpected based on our and others' findings that salicylic acid (SA) is not an important factor of local PTI in tobacco. PMC4125134.p103.s003: The innovative work of Huang et al. showed the lack of SA accumulation after inoculation of tobacco with P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p103.s004: tomato DC3000 hrcC− mutant using a SA biosensor.

PMC4125134.p105.s001: We applied PIP, a potent selective inhibitor of C4H – which is an early rate-limiting enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway – to investigate if disturbing the pathway at a key step has any effect on the outcome of PTI induction (Figure 5).

PMC4125134.p105.s001: We applied PIP, a potent selective inhibitor of C4H – which is an early rate-limiting enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway – to investigate if disturbing the pathway at a key step has any effect on the outcome of PTI induction (Figure 5).

PMC4125134.p106.s001: According to our experiments (Information S4) injected PIP seems to have a short inhibitory action on bacteria in planta, but paralelly it seems to have a longer effect on PTI development. PMC4125134.p106.s002: One possible explanation of this would be that PIP, as a quasi-irreversible inhibitor could permanently bind to the active sites of the available cinnamic-acid-4-hydroxylase (C4H) enzymes, thus blocking enzymatic activity for a longer period of time. PMC4125134.p106.s003: It is possible that only the excess free amount of PIP is metabolized soon afterwards. PMC4125134.p106.s004: However, this explanation is hypothetic and requires further studies.

PMC4125134.p107.s001: Our HR-inhibition test results indicated that a significant decrease in the HR-inhibiting ability of PTI-inducing bacteria occurred (Figure 5A, 5B). PMC4125134.p107.s002: This means that inhibition of C4H by PIP inhibited PTI as well to a certain extent, determined by our experiments. PMC4125134.p107.s003: Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p107.s004: Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.
PMC4125134.p110.s001: p-Coumarate formation is linked to PTI elicitation in tobacco

PMC4125134.p111.s001: To assess, if the actual flux of phenolic compounds follows our assumptions based on HR-inhibition tests and transcriptional data, we quantified cinnamate and p-coumarate, the second and third phenolic compounds in the phenylpropanoid pathway (as cinnamic and p-coumaric acids) by the use of a TLC-densitometry method (Figure 6A and B). PMC4125134.p111.s002: Transcriptional and HR-inhibition test data implied that C4H activity should be enhanced during PTI. PMC4125134.p111.s003: Therefore we expected a decrease in cinnamate and a parallel increase in p-coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples. PMC4125134.p111.s004: This exactly occurred in the case of treatment with PTI-inducing P. syringae pv.

PMC4125134.p112.s001: The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. PMC4125134.p112.s002: This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. PMC4125134.p112.s003: We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result. PMC4125134.p112.s004: Also, coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples were reduced when PIP was co-infiltrated with either of the two used elicitors.

PMC4125134.p112.s001: The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. PMC4125134.p112.s002: This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. PMC4125134.p112.s003: We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result. PMC4125134.p112.s004: Also, coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples were reduced when PIP was co-infiltrated with either of the two used elicitors.

PMC4125134.p113.s001: Real time RT-PCR investigation of activity changes of the identified phenylpropanoid genes in response to P. s. syringae hrcC-, flg22 and their combinations with PIP (Figure 6C) showed elevation of gene activities of certain genes in the PIP-combination treatments, as compared to the corresponding elicitor alone-treatment. PMC4125134.p113.s002: These genes were exactly the first five investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), closest to the origin of the pathway. PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. PMC4125134.p113.s004: The first four investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL) were slightly activated upon treatment solely with PIP, and of the four, activation of C4H was highest, about 3-fold.

PMC4125134.p114.s001: All in all, what our results strongly imply is that cinnamate is at least partly converted into p-coumarate during the course of PTI. PMC4125134.p114.s002: This even seems to correlate with the strength of PTI, based on our HR-inhibition tests and electrolyte-leakage experiments.

PMC4125134.p116.s001: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s002: This however raises a lot of further questions to be answered in the future. PMC4125134.p116.s003: We are planning to develop a HPLC based method to measure changes of phenolic compounds at a higher scale, to better understand the roles of these compounds in PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s004: We are especially curious about the changes in the levels of phenylalanine, salicylic acid, other phenolic acids and alcohols, flavonoids etc.

PMC4125134.p117.s001: Investigating the role of PPP in PTI, one shall remember that the presumably formed phenolic acids (i.e. coumarate, caffeate, ferulate) have been shown to have direct antimicrobial activity, which might also add to the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p117.s002: Importance of the antioxidant activity of phenylpropanoid products has been shown by Kostyn et al. in the plant–fungus interaction context. PMC4125134.p117.s003: The detected transcriptional activation of flax phenylpropanoid genes in response to Fusarium infection was supported by metabolite profiling. PMC4125134.p117.s004: Elevated antioxidant status of infected plants was verified by measuring ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) scavenging activities of identified compounds.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance.

relations.evidence_preview: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

relations.subject: heat-killed pathogenic bacteria

evidence.text: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

relations.subject: wounding

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p048.s001: Suppression subtractive hybridization was used to find genes that are activated during PTI. PMC4125134.p048.s002: For this purpose one half of the tobacco leaves was treated with a 108 CFU/ml suspension of P. syringe hrcC mutant bacteria, while the other half served as a control. PMC4125134.p048.s003: We used untreated controls at this stage, as from former experiments it was known that part of the PTI-related genes might be activated to a small extent by wounding; however we did not want to lose these potentially important genes during subtraction. PMC4125134.p048.s004: All other downstream experiments (microarray, RT-PCR, etc.) included water treated (i.e. wounded) samples for controls, and further conclusions were only drawn for PTI-related genes verified using water (or DMSO, where appropriate) treated controls.

relations.subject: inoculation

evidence.text: 156 putatively PTI-activated tobacco genes were found by suppression subtractive hybridization of samples taken 6 and 48 hours after inoculation of tobacco leaves with P. syringe hrcC- mutant bacteria.

PMC4125134.p052.s002: BLAST nucleotide similarity search (Altschul et al. 1990) was used to assign putative functions to the sequences. PMC4125134.p052.s003: Based on the identified putative functions the genes were classified into 15 groups: cell structure (2), signaling (20), cellular protection (7), cell wall (11), phenylpropanoid (9), terpenoid (4), defense related (16), heat shock (9), protein metabolism (13), transport (12), metabolism and energy (21), photosynthesis (2), miscellaneous (7), unknown function (11), no significant similarity (32). evidence.text: Potato TC-s (Tentative Contigs) could be assigned to 158 PTI-related tobacco contigs based on similarity.

relations.evidence_preview: Potato TC-s (Tentative Contigs) could be assigned to 158 PTI-related tobacco contigs based on similarity.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p062.s001: We used MAPMAN (http://mapman.gabipd.org) to visualize the differences between early (6 h) and late (48 h) sets of activated, biotic stress-related genes during PTI. PMC4125134.p062.s002: Potato TC-s (Tentative Contigs) could be assigned to 158 PTI-related tobacco contigs based on similarity. PMC4125134.p062.s003: Thirty-five of these tobacco contigs were mapped on the biotic stress pathway. PMC4125134.p062.s004: The activation data from our microarray hybridizations were then visualized by the potato biotic stress pathway in MAPMAN created by Rotter and coworkers (Information S2).

relations.subject: receptor-kinase (C90)

evidence.text: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58).

relations.evidence_preview: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p066.s001: Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI. PMC4125134.p066.s002: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs. PMC4125134.p066.s003: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58). PMC4125134.p066.s004: Interestingly, contigs of genes representing three calcium-dependent proteins were also found (C53 - calcium-dependent protein kinase 4, C95 - Avr9/Cf-9 rapidly elicited protein 31, C125 - annexin-like protein).

relations.subject: phenylpropanoid

evidence.text: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library.

relations.evidence_preview: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p071.s001: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library. PMC4125134.p071.s002: Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well.

relations.subject: PAL

evidence.text: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL).

relations.evidence_preview: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well. PMC4125134.p071.s005: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL). PMC4125134.p071.s006: Activation of the genes encoding the enzymes of the next stages of the pathway, such as ortho-methyltransferases (OMT) and ferulate-5-hydroxylase (F5H) was also significant. PMC4125134.p071.s007: Three isoenzymes of the peroxidases that oxidize the aromatic alcoholic compounds into lignins were also activated.

entities.selected_description: The cellular process in which a signal is conveyed to trigger a change in the activity or state of a cell. Signal transduction begins with reception of a signal (e.g. a ligand binding to a receptor or receptor activation by a stimulus such as light), or for signal transduction in the absence of ligand, signal-withdrawal or the activity of a constitutively active receptor. Signal transduction ends with regulation of a downstream cellular process, e.g. regulation of transcription or regulation of a metabolic process. Signal transduction covers signaling from receptors located on the surface of the cell and signaling via molecules located within the cell. For signaling between cells, signal transduction is restricted to events at and within the receiving cell.

sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance.
Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.
Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria.

Analysis of hybridization data showed that in the early (6 hpi) phase of PTI, among others, genes of peroxidases, signalling elements, heat shock proteins and secondary metabolites were upregulated, while at the late phase (48 hpi) the group of proteolysis genes was newly activated. Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3).
All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well.
Activation of the genes encoding the enzymes of the next stages of the pathway, such as ortho-methyltransferases (OMT) and ferulate-5-hydroxylase (F5H) was also significant.

Inhibition of C4H activity and PTI development by PIP

We applied PIP, a potent selective inhibitor of C4H – which is an early rate-limiting enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway – to investigate if disturbing the pathway at a key step has any effect on the outcome of PTI induction (Figure 5).
According to our experiments (Information S4) injected PIP seems to have a short inhibitory action on bacteria in planta, but paralelly it seems to have a longer effect on PTI development.
This means that inhibition of C4H by PIP inhibited PTI as well to a certain extent, determined by our experiments.
Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.
p-Coumarate formation is linked to PTI elicitation in tobacco
Transcriptional and HR-inhibition test data implied that C4H activity should be enhanced during PTI.

However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control.

A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate.

The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result.
We can say that these TLC derived data correlated well with data from HR-inhibition experiments (Figure 5).

All in all, what our results strongly imply is that cinnamate is at least partly converted into p-coumarate during the course of PTI.
Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI.
